primary_diagnosis.name: Large mature furuncle (staphylococcal skin abscess) of the posterior thigh with surrounding cellulitis and early systemic features

pathophysiology_summary: A furuncle is a deep bacterial infection of a hair follicle, usually Staphylococcus aureus (including community-associated MRSA in patients with recurrent disease and prior MRSA, as here), that extends into the dermis and subcutaneous tissue to form a walled-off collection of pus (abscess). As the abscess matures it becomes fluctuant and 'points' at the skin surface (the yellow-white head). Surrounding erythema, warmth and induration indicate accompanying cellulitis; low-grade fever, malaise and tender regional (inguinal) lymphadenopathy indicate early systemic and lymphatic spread. The definitive treatment of a fluctuant, pointing abscess is source control by incision and drainage — systemic antibiotics alone cannot penetrate a walled-off purulent collection. Antibiotics (MRSA-active where MRSA is likely) are an adjunct for the surrounding cellulitis and systemic features. Untreated, the infection can extend to widespread cellulitis, bacteraemia/sepsis, or (rarely) necrotising soft-tissue infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly enlarging, painful, red, firm nodule that has softened centrally with a pointing head — the classic evolution of a follicular abscess.
Spreading erythema and warmth are present, but the fluctuant pointing centre indicates a drainable abscess is also present — not cellulitis alone.
Can present as an inflamed nodule, but there is no history of a long-standing painless subcutaneous lump preceding the acute event.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the key move is recognising a probable drainable abscess rather than treating it as simple cellulitis.

differential.diagnosis: furuncle / cutaneous abscess with surrounding cellulitis
differential.evidence_basis: Pointing fluctuant lesion plus spreading peri-lesional erythema plus low-grade fever and malaise in a patient with recurrent staph/MRSA.
Considered because of size and inflammation, but a carbuncle has multiple coalescing loculi and several draining sinus openings; here there is a single locus of pointing.

Must be actively screened for and excluded: pain out of proportion, rapidly spreading dusky/purple discolouration, blistering, crepitus, or systemic toxicity/shock would mandate emergency escalation. Not present here (stable observations, no necrotic features), but the safety net must cover it.
A solitary acute thigh lesion without chronic double comedones, recurrent tunnelling or scarring in intertriginous sites (axillae/groin) is not hidradenitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The important-not-to-miss line is necrotising infection; recognising it as the escalation trigger (not the baseline diagnosis) is the mark of correct reasoning.

differential.diagnosis: large mature furuncle (staphylococcal abscess) with surrounding cellulitis, early systemic features, likely MRSA
differential.evidence_basis: Whole constellation plus recurrent-staph/prior-MRSA history; requires same-day source control (incision and drainage) and MRSA-active antibiotics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot drain the abscess or exclude a deeper collection; the correct output is same-day in-person assessment for drainage, not remote antibiotic-only management.

red_flags.description: Spreading peri-lesional erythema and warmth beyond the abscess edge — surrounding cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates the infection is extending into surrounding tissue; combined with a drainable collection this needs same-day in-person care and antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to extensive cellulitis, lymphangitis and bacteraemia if not drained and treated.

red_flags.description: New low-grade fever and systemic malaise — early systemic involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic symptoms accompanying a soft-tissue infection raise the urgency from routine to same-day and lower the threshold for escalation if they worsen.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early sepsis; delayed drainage allows systemic deterioration.

red_flags.description: Fluctuant, pointing abscess — a drainable purulent collection requiring source control
red_flags.clinical_significance: A walled-off collection cannot be cleared by antibiotics alone; incision and drainage is the definitive treatment and cannot be done via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Antibiotic-only management fails, pain and collection persist, and the infection may enlarge or rupture uncontrolled.

red_flags.description: Features of necrotising soft-tissue infection (pain out of proportion, dusky/blistering skin, crepitus, systemic toxicity) — absent here but must be screened
red_flags.clinical_significance: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency; any of these features mandates immediate 000/ED rather than routine drainage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed necrotising infection is rapidly fatal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a fluctuant, pointing abscess with oral antibiotics (or home warm compresses) alone and omitting the need for incision and drainage — antibiotics do not penetrate walled-off pus.
Choosing a plain anti-staphylococcal beta-lactam (flucloxacillin/cephalexin) when the recurrent-staph and prior-MRSA history make community MRSA likely, so MRSA-active cover is needed.
Reassuring the patient it is 'just a boil' and advising self-care only, missing the surrounding cellulitis, systemic features and the need for same-day source control.
Failing to screen for pain out of proportion, dusky skin, blistering or crepitus, which would upgrade this from same-day drainage to an emergency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether the lesion is truly fluctuant / the exact depth and size of the collection (requires palpation)
Presence and tenderness of regional inguinal lymphadenopathy (requires palpation)
Precise vital signs — blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and oxygen saturation
Exclusion of a deeper or necrotising soft-tissue infection
Wound microbiology / MRSA status on this presentation (requires a swab at drainage)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP or urgent care) for incision and drainage with a wound swab, plus consideration of MRSA-active oral antibiotics for the surrounding cellulitis; advise immediate ED/000 if systemic toxicity or necrotising features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the abscess itself (the pointing fluctuant lesion is characteristic on video); the real risk is UNDER-action — managing remotely with antibiotics alone and not arranging same-day drainage, or missing an evolving necrotising infection.
